goals.name: Submitting review-ready work
goals.description: Before requesting review, the author's job is to make the change cheap to review: scope it to one self-contained concern of a reviewable size, describe what changed and why so a reviewer can build an accurate mental model without reverse-engineering the diff, and hand it off ready and traceable to its motivating issue. This is a process habit about how work is packaged and handed off, not a hunt for code defects. Modern code review is lightweight and iterative (Bacchelli and Bird, ICSE 2013; Sadowski et al., ICSE-SEIP 2018), and its effectiveness degrades when changes are large or unexplained: the SmartBear/Cisco 2,500-review study found defect-detection yield is highest around 200-400 changed lines and falls off well beyond that, and Google's CL author guidance asks for small self-contained changes plus a description whose first line is a specific imperative summary of what, with a body explaining why. Every criterion is judged only from observable PR artifacts the agent actually receives in metadata.json (pr_number, pr_url, repository_full_name, source_branch, target_branch, commit_sha, and — only when metadata.enriched is true — title, body, is_draft, state, additions, deletions, changed_files, author, commits[]) plus the file-path table in diff_summary.md. Issue references are not delivered as a structured field; they are parsed from the title, body, and branch name. The agent never invents signals it does not receive — there is no CI status, no mergeability, no build result, and no structured linked-issue field in its context.

practices.name: Scope the change to one reviewable concern
practices.criteria:
Assess whether this change is packaged at a size and spread that keeps review effective, framed as a workflow habit about how the change is batched for a reviewer — never as a code-defect claim, and never exceeding MAJOR on size alone.

## Source of counts (single authoritative source)
Use ONLY the integer fields metadata.additions, metadata.deletions, and metadata.changed_files. Compute net changed lines as additions + deletions. Do NOT parse diff_stat.txt or estimate counts from the diff text, and state the exact figures you used. NOTE: these counts are raw DB totals — no generated/vendored exclusion is applied to them. Because of that, you MUST sanity-check them against the file-path table in diff_summary.md before bucketing: if the table shows that the bulk of the changed files (or the bulk of the +Lines) are generated or vendored — dependency lock files (e.g. *.lock, *-lock.*, go.sum), code-generation output (*.gen.*, *.pb.*, OpenAPI specs, router/route trees), committed build artifacts (e.g. dist/), snapshots, and DB migrations — then the raw counts overstate true review burden; in that case DOWNGRADE the severity by one bucket, or emit NOT_APPLICABLE when generated/vendored paths visibly dominate the table.

## Severity (fixed — apply exactly, no discretion)
Buckets on net changed lines (additions + deletions), with a file-spread override (Google: a 200-line change across 50 files is too large): <=200 lines AND <=10 files -> POSITIVE (well-sized, single batch). 201-400 lines AND <=10 files -> INFO (acceptable upper band per the SmartBear yield curve). 401-800 lines OR 11-20 files -> MINOR (consider splitting into self-contained changes). >800 lines OR >20 files -> MAJOR (review effectiveness materially degraded; recommend a refactor-first or stacked split). Identical counts (after the same generated-dominance check) must always yield identical severity.

## Grounding gate (mandatory)
If metadata.enriched is false, or metadata.additions / metadata.deletions / metadata.changed_files are absent, emit NOT_APPLICABLE — the PR could not be enriched from the database and its size fields are not present; never treat missing counts as zero. Every finding MUST state the exact additions, deletions, total, and changed_files it used. If the diff_summary.md file-path table shows generated/vendored paths dominate, emit NOT_APPLICABLE rather than guessing.

## Verdict & abstention
Emit POSITIVE/NEGATIVE only when the integer counts are present (enriched=true). Otherwise emit NOT_APPLICABLE. Never assert compile/runtime/correctness behavior from the size figures.

## Verdicts
- POSITIVE: additions + deletions is <=200 across <=10 changed files, with the exact integer counts stated, the diff_summary.md table confirms the bulk is hand-written, and the touched files cohere around a single concern.
- NEGATIVE: additions + deletions exceeds 800, or changed_files exceeds 20, in one review request — large enough to reduce review effectiveness and likely bundling more than one concern — with the bulk confirmed non-generated in the diff_summary.md table.
- NOT_APPLICABLE: metadata.enriched is false or the size integers are absent, OR the diff_summary.md file-path table shows generated/vendored paths dominate the change, so review-size cannot be meaningfully assessed.

practices.name: Describe what changed and why
practices.criteria:
Assess whether the PR title and body let a reviewer understand the change without reading the diff first, using ONLY metadata.title and metadata.body. Do NOT inspect the diff to invent rationale on the author's behalf. The description is ADEQUATE iff (a) the title is a specific imperative summary of WHAT changed — not 'fix', 'update', 'wip', 'changes', a bare branch slug, an issue-ref-only such as 'fixes #12', or a generic phrase Google names as inadequate such as 'Fix bug' or 'Phase 1' — AND (b) the body states the WHY/motivation in at least one full sentence. To claim WHY is present you MUST quote the exact rationale sentence verbatim from metadata.body; an empty body, an unmodified template, or a body that only restates the title means WHY is absent. Guidance is feed-forward and template-shaped: name the missing element and show the heading to add (e.g. '## Why'), and talk about the description, never grade the author.

## Severity (fixed — apply exactly, no discretion)
MAJOR = empty body OR unmodified template OR body only restates the title (no quotable WHY) AND metadata.changed_files >1. MINOR = body has some context but no quotable WHY sentence, OR a generic title with an otherwise-informative body. INFO = adequate body but a weak/generic title only. POSITIVE (no severity) = a specific imperative title AND a verbatim-quoted rationale sentence. Never escalate above MAJOR on description quality alone.

## Grounding gate (mandatory)
If metadata.enriched is false, or metadata.title / metadata.body are absent, emit NOT_APPLICABLE — the PR could not be enriched from the database, so its description fields are not present; never read a missing body as an empty one. POSITIVE and any 'why present' claim REQUIRE a verbatim quoted sentence from the body supplying motivation. A 'no rationale' NEGATIVE requires that the quoted body contains no such sentence (quote it, or state it is empty/template).

## Verdict & abstention
Emit POSITIVE/NEGATIVE only when enriched=true and the deciding title/body text is quotable. Otherwise emit NOT_APPLICABLE. Never assert compile/runtime/correctness behavior from the description.

## Verdicts
- POSITIVE: The title is a specific imperative summary of the change and the body contains a verbatim-quotable sentence explaining the motivation, so a reviewer can orient before reading code.
- NEGATIVE: The body is empty / an unmodified template / only restates the title, leaving the why to be inferred from the diff; adding a sentence on the problem this solves would let a reviewer understand intent without reverse-engineering the change.
- NOT_APPLICABLE: metadata.enriched is false or metadata.title / metadata.body are absent, so description quality cannot be meaningfully judged.

practices.name: Hand the change off ready and traceable
practices.criteria:
Assess whether the change is handed off as ready (not a work-in-progress) and is traceable to its motivating issue, using ONLY metadata fields actually present: metadata.is_draft, metadata.title, metadata.body, and metadata.source_branch. There is NO CI-status, mergeability, or structured linked-issue field in the context — never claim build/CI/merge state or a linked-issue field; doing so invalidates the finding. Two sub-checks. READINESS: a title token 'WIP'/'DRAFT'/'do not merge' while metadata.is_draft is false is a mixed-signal handoff; is_draft being true on its own is a legitimate state and only informational. TRACEABILITY: scan metadata.title, metadata.body, and metadata.source_branch for an issue reference (a closing keyword + number such as close/closes/fix/fixes/resolve/resolves #N, a tracker URL, or an issue id embedded in the branch slug) and quote the matched token; a plain mention without a closing keyword is traceable but does not auto-close. Combine by emitting the most severe of the two sub-results; guidance is feed-forward (show the 'Closes #N' syntax or note the mixed WIP signal), never a grade of the author.

## Severity (fixed — apply exactly, no discretion)
POSITIVE = is_draft is false AND no WIP/DRAFT token in the title AND a quotable issue reference exists in title/body/branch. INFO = is_draft is true (informational only). MINOR = title carries WIP/DRAFT/'do not merge' while is_draft is false, OR a plain issue mention without a closing keyword, OR no issue reference anywhere on a feature/fix-scoped change (changed_files >1) whose title is not a trivial chore. MAJOR = both a mixed WIP-signal handoff AND no issue reference on a feature/fix-scoped change. Never exceed MAJOR.

## Grounding gate (mandatory)
If metadata.enriched is false or metadata.is_draft is absent, emit NOT_APPLICABLE for readiness — the draft flag is not present. A traceability POSITIVE MUST quote the exact reference token (e.g. 'Closes #482' or branch '748-feat-...'). A 'no reference' claim is made only after naming all three surfaces scanned (title, body, branch). A readiness NEGATIVE MUST quote the offending title token alongside is_draft=false. For traceability, emit NOT_APPLICABLE when no closing-keyword or #N token appears in title/body/branch AND the change is a trivial chore (dependency bump / formatting) by its title — the agent cannot otherwise know whether a tracker is in use. If neither a readiness signal nor a groundable traceability signal is present, emit NOT_APPLICABLE overall.

## Verdict & abstention
Emit POSITIVE/NEGATIVE only when the deciding flag or reference token is quotable from the case context (enriched=true for readiness). Otherwise emit NOT_APPLICABLE. Never assert CI/build/merge readiness — those signals are not in the metadata.

## Verdicts
- POSITIVE: is_draft is false with no WIP/DRAFT token in the title, and a quotable closing reference (e.g. 'Closes #482', or the issue id in the branch slug) ties the change to its motivating issue.
- NEGATIVE: The change is feature-scoped (changed_files >1, non-chore title) yet carries no issue reference in its title, body, or branch name; adding a 'Closes #<issue>' line in the description would let the work be traced back to the need it addresses.
- NOT_APPLICABLE: metadata.enriched is false / is_draft is absent (readiness cannot be checked), or no closing-keyword or #N token appears in title/body/branch and the change is a trivial chore by its title, so handoff readiness and traceability cannot be assessed.

goals.name: Acting on review feedback
goals.description: This goal develops the habit of closing the review loop on a pull request: when a reviewer leaves a substantive inline comment on a diff line — a question or a change request — the work visibly takes it up, either through a later inline reply on the same file and line that names what changed (or why not), or through a follow-up commit whose message references the same file or a concrete term from the comment. Feedback only changes the work when it is acted upon: Hattie and Timperley (2007) frame review as feed-back plus a feed-forward "Where to next?" step that has no effect in a vacuum, and Shute (2008) defines formative feedback as information presented in response to the learner's action, so uptake is what makes it function. In code review this uptake is also a recognized signal of review usefulness — a comment that triggers a code change is the usefulness signal, and review iterates until comments are addressed (Bosu, Greiler and Bird, MSR 2015). The agent assesses ONLY observable uptake in the materialized PR context, which carries exclusively inline diff-anchored review comments (each with its file path, line, body, author login, and created_at) and an unordered list of follow-up commit messages (sha plus subject, with NO commit date). It therefore reasons only about the inline review thread and the presence of a textually-matching follow-up commit; it never judges whether the resulting code is correct, never reads an unseen reviewer's intent, never relies on a 'resolved' flag or top-level conversation summary the context does not carry, and never infers uptake from time-ordering, since commit messages carry no comparable timestamp. Because a freshly-opened PR usually carries no substantive inline reviewer comments, the default verdict is NOT_APPLICABLE, so the practice never fabricates a dangling-thread finding.

practices.name: Substantive inline review comments are taken up rather than left dangling
practices.criteria:
Assess, from the inline review-comment thread in comments.json[] and the follow-up commit list in metadata.json commits[] ONLY, whether substantive inline reviewer comments have been taken up. Every comment in comments.json[] is an inline comment anchored to a diff line (it carries path, line, body, author, created_at); the materialized context does NOT carry top-level/conversation-tab comments or any 'resolved' flag, so reason only about these inline comments. A substantive comment is a reviewer question or change request. Identify reviewer comments as those whose author login differs from metadata.json 'author' (the PR author). Apply this observable exclusion filter and skip the comment if any clause holds: (i) its body contains the marker string '<!-- hephaestus-diff-note -->' or '<!-- hephaestus' (Hephaestus's own posted review notes), (ii) its author login ends in 'bot' or '[bot]' or contains 'hephaestus', or (iii) it is a bare approval or acknowledgement ('LGTM', '+1', 'approved', a lone thumbs-up). For each remaining substantive inline reviewer comment, treat it as taken up if EITHER (a) a LATER entry in comments.json[] on the SAME path and line, authored by the PR author, replies to its concern — quote the reply — OR (b) metadata.json commits[] contains a follow-up commit whose message provides an OBSERVABLE textual anchor to the comment: the commit message names the SAME file/path the comment is on, or quotes/paraphrases a concrete term that appears in the comment body — quote BOTH the comment phrase and the matching commit-message phrase. Do NOT infer uptake from commit presence or time-ordering alone: commits[] carries no date and is unordered relative to comment created_at, so a commit that shares no path and no term with the comment is NOT evidence of uptake. Do NOT judge whether the resulting code is correct. Frame all wording about the open loop in the work ('this thread is still open'), never about the contributor ('you ignored the reviewer'), and describe state rather than asserting causal outcomes ('the thread is closed', not 'the reviewer's effort changed the work'). POSITIVE: at least one substantive inline reviewer comment is quotable and EVERY such comment has either a quotable later same-path same-line author reply addressing it or a quotable follow-up commit whose message textually anchors to it (shared path or shared term, both quoted), so every thread is closed. NEGATIVE: at least one quotable substantive inline reviewer comment has no later same-path same-line author reply AND no follow-up commit whose message textually anchors to it; name the single most consequential open thread, quote the comment, and point forward to replying in-thread or pushing a fix. NOT_APPLICABLE: after the exclusion filter, comments.json[] contains no substantive inline reviewer comments (the common case for a newly-opened PR) — it is empty, or holds only Hephaestus/bot output, approvals, or 'LGTM' — so there is no reviewer input whose uptake could be assessed; also NOT_APPLICABLE when the only candidate link between a comment and a commit is timing or mere commit presence with no shared path or term, since uptake then cannot be confirmed from the artifact.

## Verdicts
- POSITIVE: At least one substantive inline reviewer comment is quotable, and every such comment has either a quotable later author reply on the same path and line addressing its concern, or a quotable follow-up commit whose message textually anchors to it (shared file path or shared concrete term, both quoted).
- NEGATIVE: At least one quotable substantive inline reviewer comment (question or change request) has no later same-path same-line author reply and no follow-up commit whose message textually anchors to it (no shared path, no shared term), leaving the thread open.
- NOT_APPLICABLE: After excluding Hephaestus/bot output, approvals, and 'LGTM', comments.json[] holds no substantive inline reviewer comments, so there is no reviewer input whose uptake could be assessed; or the only candidate uptake is a commit linked by timing or mere presence with no shared path or term, which the artifact cannot confirm.

goals.name: Writing issues a maintainer can act on
goals.description: An issue is the unit of planned work. This goal asks whether an issue — read on its own from its title, body, labels, and comments — gives a maintainer enough to start work without a round-trip for clarification. It draws on two practitioner literatures: empirical bug-report research (what reporters omit that developers most need) and the INVEST checklist for backlog items (one cohesive concern with a checkable outcome). The agent observes only the issue text and labels the provider materializes — it never runs code, never judges whether a reported defect is real, and never judges whether a requested feature is worth building — so every criterion is keyed to quotable text or the labels array. Feedback is formative and feed-forward: name the single missing element and the heading to add, and treat the gap as an improvable habit rather than a grade on the reporter.

practices.name: The issue states a problem a maintainer can act on
practices.criteria:
Assess whether this issue, read alone, gives a maintainer enough to start work — judged ONLY from the title, body, and labels[] in metadata.json plus comments[].body. Never run code, never claim the described defect is real or that the steps reproduce, and never judge whether a requested feature is worth building; you assess only whether the report is act-on-able as written.

STEP 1 - Classify the kind of work, vocabulary-agnostically. Do NOT rely on a specific label namespace (such as type::) or on a template heading existing. Classify as BUG from any label whose name case-insensitively contains 'bug', 'defect', or 'fix', or from a body that asserts a malfunction (something does not work, errors, crashes, or produces wrong output). Classify as STORY from any label whose name case-insensitively contains 'enhancement', 'feature', or 'story', or from a body that requests a new capability. If a recognizable bug-report template structure is present, treat as BUG. Reserve NOT_APPLICABLE ('work kind not declared') only for bodies that are purely a question or open discussion with no asserted defect and no requested capability — this guards a discussion thread from being asked for repro steps; it does NOT excuse an untyped issue that nonetheless asserts a malfunction.

STEP 2 - Check the elements the kind of work needs, and for EVERY element you mark present you MUST quote the exact span from the body or a comment; an element you cannot quote is counted absent.
- BUG needs: observed/actual behavior, expected behavior, and steps to reproduce. Steps-to-reproduce is the highest-weighted element (empirically the element developers most need and reporters most often omit). Note environment/version when the defect is platform- or version-sensitive, but do not require it otherwise.
- STORY needs: who benefits / which user, what capability, and why / the value ('so that...').
Also check the title: POSITIVE expects a title specific enough for a maintainer to locate the work — not a bare branch slug, an empty or unmodified-template title, or a vague phrase ('it is broken', 'fix bug'). A solution-phrased title ('Add retry to upload') is acceptable when it is specific; do not penalize a title merely for naming a solution.

Guidance must be feed-forward (Hattie & Timperley 'Where to next?'): name the single most consequential missing element and show the heading the reporter could add (e.g. '### Steps to reproduce', or a 'so that...' value clause), never rewrite their content for them, never cite academic findings to the reporter, never praise or grade the reporter.

## Verdicts
- POSITIVE: The kind of work is identifiable (from a label, a template, or an asserted defect/capability in the body), and every element it needs is quotable: for a bug, a quotable observed result is separated from a quotable expected result and a sequenced steps-to-reproduce section is present; for a story, who/what/why are all quotable. The title is specific enough to locate the work, so a maintainer can orient and start without a clarification round-trip.
- NEGATIVE: An element the kind of work needs is not quotable from the body — most consequentially, a defect issue (typed OR asserted in prose) with no quotable steps to reproduce, or a symptom stated with no expected-behavior contrast, or a story missing its value ('so that...'). The feedback names the single missing element and shows the heading to add, so the report becomes act-on-able next time.
- NOT_APPLICABLE: The body is purely a question or open discussion with no asserted defect and no requested capability, so there is no work-kind to check the report against and no feedback is given. This abstention prevents demanding repro steps of a discussion thread; it does not apply to an untyped issue that still asserts a malfunction.

practices.name: The issue is scoped to a single concern
practices.criteria:
Judge whether this issue is one cohesive deliverable, from the body, labels[], and the sub_issues_total/sub_issues_completed rollup ONLY. Never assert technical feasibility, hour-estimates, or design correctness; never praise the author's competence.

STEP 1 - If the issue is purely a question or open discussion with no concrete deliverable (explicit support/discussion label or interrogative-only body), this practice cannot be assessed -> NOT_APPLICABLE.

STEP 2 - SINGLE CONCERN: treat the issue as multi-concern ONLY when you can quote >=2 spans that each describe an independently-shippable deliverable (heterogeneous task-list items, an 'and also...' that introduces a separable change, distinct verb+object pairs targeting unrelated areas). A single feature decomposed into implementation sub-steps is ONE concern — do not over-split; a sub-issue rollup (sub_issues_total > 0) is a legitimate way to track sub-steps of one concern, not evidence of bundling.

Guidance is feed-forward: if multi-concern, suggest splitting into N linked issues named for each concern you quoted, so each can be picked up and finished on its own. Keep to the single most consequential split rather than enumerating every clause.

## Verdicts
- POSITIVE: The body describes one cohesive deliverable, and any sub-issue rollup reads as sub-steps of that one concern rather than separable work. The issue can be picked up, worked, and closed as a unit, satisfying INVEST Small/Independent.
- NEGATIVE: The body bundles >=2 separately-shippable deliverables, each quoted. The feedback names the concerns to split into linked issues, so the next issue is scoped to be estimated, prioritized, and closed cleanly.
- NOT_APPLICABLE: The issue reads as a question, support request, or open discussion with no concrete deliverable, so single-concern scoping does not apply and no feedback is given.

practices.name: The issue defines a checkable outcome
practices.criteria:
Judge whether this issue defines an outcome a maintainer could verify as done, from the body, labels[], and comments[].body ONLY. Never assert technical feasibility or design correctness; never praise the author's competence.

STEP 1 - If the issue is purely a question or open discussion with no concrete deliverable, this practice cannot be assessed -> NOT_APPLICABLE.

STEP 2 - CHECKABLE OUTCOME: a checkable outcome is satisfied by ANY of the following, each requiring a quotable span: an explicit acceptance-criteria or Given-When-Then block; for a story, a who/what/why that makes the outcome testable; for a bug, a quotable expected-behavior, which serves as the implicit acceptance criterion (the fix is verified when observed behavior matches the stated expected behavior). If none of these is quotable, 'done' is undefined.

Guidance is feed-forward: if no checkable outcome is quotable, show a short Given-When-Then or acceptance-criteria template, or — for a bug — point to stating the expected behavior that a fix must satisfy. Keep to this single gap.

## Verdicts
- POSITIVE: A checkable outcome is quotable — an acceptance-criteria or Given-When-Then block, or a story's who/what/why, or a bug's expected behavior that the fix must satisfy. A maintainer can tell when the issue is done, satisfying INVEST Testable.
- NEGATIVE: No checkable outcome is quotable, so 'done' is undefined: a story with no who/what/why, or a bug with no expected-behavior contrast, and no acceptance-criteria block. The feedback shows a Given-When-Then template or points to stating the expected behavior, so the next issue is verifiable.
- NOT_APPLICABLE: The issue reads as a question, support request, or open discussion with no concrete deliverable, so a checkable outcome does not apply and no feedback is given. This abstention prevents demanding acceptance criteria of a discussion thread.
